title: Smt Chandrawwa vs Channawwa W/O Shankarappa Angadi on 20 July, 2012
body: Smt Chandrawwa vs Channawwa W/O Shankarappa Angadi on 20 July, 2012 Author: B.V.Nagarathna Bench: B.V. Nagarathna 1 IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA CIRCUIT BENCH AT DHARWAD DATED THIS THE 20TH DAY OF JULY 2012 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MRS. JUSTICE B.V. NAGARATHNA R.S.A.No.5292/2008 (DEC/INJ) BETWEEN: SMT CHANDRAWWA, W/O KASHAPPA MADAR, Age:43 YEARS, OCC HOUSEHOLD WORK R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL, DIST: BAGALKOT. ... APPELLANT (By SRI.SHRIHARSH A. NEELOPANT, ADV.) AND 1. CHANNAWWA W/O SHANKARAPPA ANGADI Age: 44 YEARS, OCC: AGRICULTURE. R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL, DIST: BAGALKOT. 2. BOURAWWA D/O SHANKARAPPA ANGADI Age: 26 YEARS, OCC STUDENT R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL, DIST: BAGALKOT. 3. LAXMIBAI D/O SHANKRAPPA ANGADI Age: 23 YEARS, OCC STUDENT, R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL. DIST: BAGALKOT. 2 4. SHOBHA D/O SHANKRAPPA ANGADI Age: 21 YEARS. OCC STUDENT R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL DIST: BAGALKOT. 5. MUTHAPPA S/O SHANKRAPPA ANGADI Age: 19 YEARS. OCC STUDENT, R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL DIST: BAGALKOT. 6. KRISHNAPPA S/O RAMA MANG Age: 61 YEARS. OCC AGRIL. R/O MANTUR TQ MUDHOL, DIST: BAGALKOT. 7. SMT GANGAWWA W/O PEERAPPA CHAMBAR Age: 53 YEARS. OCC AGRIL. R/O MANTUR TQ MUDHOL, DIST: BAGALKOT. 8. SMT JAYAWWA W/O YAMANAPPA MANNYAGOL Age: 33 YEARS. OCC AGRIL. R/O MANTUR TQ MUDHOL. DIST: BAGALKOT. 9. SMT CHANDRAWWA PEERAPPA CHAMBER Age: 31 YEARS. OCC AGRIL R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL DIST: BAGALKOT. 10. SMT KASHAWWA PEERAPPA CHAMBAR Age: 29 YEARS. OCC AGRIL, R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL DIST: BAGALKOT. 3 11. SMT YALLAWA W/O PEERAPPA CHAMBAR Age: 29 YEARS, OCC AGRIL R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL DIST: BAGALKOT. 12. SRI LAXMAN S/O PEERAPPA CHAMBAR Age: 25 YEARS. OCC AGRIL. R/O MANTUR, TQ MUDHOL. ... RESPONDENTS (By SRI. SHIVARAJ P. MUDHOL, ADV. FOR R.1 TO R.5 AND R.7 TO R.12,) THIS RSA IS FILED UNDER SECTION 100 OF CPC , PRAYING TO SET ASIDE THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE PASSED IN REGULAR APPEAL NO.94/2006 DATED 31.7.2008 PASSED BY THE CIVIL JUDGE (SR.DN) AT JAMKHANDI, AND ETC. THIS APPEAL COMING ON FOR HEARING THIS DAY, THE COURT., DELIVERED THE FOLLOWING: JUDGMENT This appeal is filed by defendant-2 in O. S.31 /2005 assailing the judgment and decree passed by the I Appellate Court in R.A.94/2006 dated 31.7.2008 passed by the Civil Judge, (Sr.Dn.), Jamkhandi, by which the judgment and decree of the Principal Civil Judge (Jr.Dn), Mudhol, in O. S.31 /2005 dated 31.3.2006 was set aside. For the sake of convenience, the parties shall be referred to in terms of their status before the trial Court. 4 2. Respondents 1 to 5 herein are the plaintiffs who filed a suit seeking declaration, injunction and for rectification of the revenue records in respect of R.S.206/ 8 measuring 1 acre 32 guntas situated at Mantur village. According to them, the deceased husband of plaintiff-1 and the father of other plaintiffs had purchased the suit property from its owner-defendant-1 on 13.11.1981 for a valuable consideration and since the said date they had been in peaceful possession and enjoyment of the suit land. That in the sale deed, the property was wrongly mentioned as R.S.188/ 1 . However, on 23.4.1971 defendant-1 sold R.S.188/ 1 measuring 1 acre 22 guntas, situated in the same village to defendant-3 and that defendant-3 was in possession of the said property. Though R.S.188/ 1 was purchased by defendant-3 on 23.4.1971, there was no mutation made in the revenue records and by mistake while executing the sale deed on 13.11.1981 R.S.188/ 1 was mentioned instead of R.S.206/ 8 . 3. That defendant-3 had filed O.S.265/1991 seeking a declaration that he is the owner in possession of 5 R.S.188/ 1 measuring 1 acre 22 guntas of land and that a declaration was granted in that regard and thereafter, he had got his name entered in the revenue records. That plaintiffs sought for change in the mutation entries with regard to R.S.206/ 8 . At that time, the Tahasildar, Mudhol, intimated in writing that the name of defendant-2 was found with regard to R.S.206/ 8 in the revenue records. It is under those circumstances, that the plaintiffs filed the suit seeking declaration of title, injunction and rectification in the revenue records. 4. After service of notice from the trial Court, defendants 1 to 3 appeared and filed their separate written statements. Defendant-2 denied the right, title and interest of the plaintiffs with regard to the suit schedule property i.e. R.S.206/2008, by stating that there was no sale effected by defendant-1 in the name of the husband of plaintiff-1 on 23.4.1971. It was also denied that R.S.188/ 1 and R.S.206/ 8 (suit schedule properties) were owned by defendant-1. Defendant-2 stated that she was not aware of O.S.265/1991 being filed by defendant-3 and that the suit 6 being decreed and that there were mutation entries made in the name of defendant-3 thereafter with regard to the R.S.188/ 1 . It was contended by defendant-2 that she is the owner in actual possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule property bearing R.S.206/ 8 measuring 1 acre 32 guntas and that the said property came to his share at a partition held in the year 1992 and that she has been in possession of the said property since then and had effected mutation entries in her name. She also stated that the judgment and decree passed in O.S.265/1991 is not binding on her and therefore, sought for dismissal of the suit. 5. Defendant-1 has admitted the fact that he had sold the property to deceased Shankareppa - husband of plaintiff No.1 in the year 1981 and that the plaintiffs were in actual possession and enjoyment of the suit schedule property since then. He also admitted that, at the time of execution of the sale deed the survey number was wrongly mentioned as R.S.188/ 1 and that correct survey number is R.S.206/ 8 . The 1-defendant also admitted the decree passed in O.S.265/1991. Defendant No.1 7 categorically stated that defendant-2 was in no way concerned with the said land and he had illegally mutated his name in the revenue records. 6. Defendant No.3 did not file any written statement. On the basis of the rival pleadings, the trial Court framed the following issues: i. Whether the plaintiffs prove that the suit property has been purchased in the year 1981 from defendant No.1 and since from the date of purchase till today they are in actual peaceful possession and enjoyment of the suit land and they are the owners for the suit land as being alleged? ii. Whether plaintiffs prove that the defendant No.2 got mutated his name in the record of rights without any base and without the knowledge of the plaintiffs during the pendency of O.S.No.265/1991? iii. Whether the defendant No.2 prove that the judgment and decree being passed in O.S.No.265/191 does not bind on him? iv. Whether this Court has got pecuniary jurisdiction to try this suit? v. Whether the plaintiffs are entitled for the relief as being prayed for? vi. What order or decree? 8 7. In support of their case, the plaintiffs let-in the evidence of PWs.1 to 3 and produced ten documents, which were marked as Ex.P.1 to Ex.P.10. While the defendants let- in evidence of DW.1 to DW.5 and produced 25 documents which were marked as Ex.D.1 to D.25. 8. On the basis of the said evidence, the trial Court answered issue Nos.1, 2, 4 and 5 in the negative and issue No.3 in the affirmative and dismissed the suit of the plaintiffs with costs. The plaintiffs being aggrieved by the said judgment and decree, preferred R.A.94/2006 before the I Appellate Court which formulated the following points for its consideration: "1) PÉ¼À £ÁåAiÀiÁ®AiÀÄzÀªÀgÀÄ ¥Àæ²ßvÀ wÃ¥ÀÄð PÁ£ÀÆ£ÀÄ¨Á»gÀªÁVgÀÄªÀzÉÃ? 2) AiÀiÁªÀ DzÉÃ±À? " and answered Point No.1 in the affirmative and allowed the appeal and decreed the suit of the plaintiffs. 9. It is against the said judgment and decree of the I Appellate Court that defendant-2 has preferred this appeal. 9 10. I have heard the learned Counsel for the appellant and the learned Counsel for respondents 1 to 5 and respondents 7 to 12. 11. It is contended on behalf of the appellant that the suit schedule property, viz., R.S.206/ 8 fell to the share of the appellant at a partition which took place in the year 1992 and that she has been the owner in possession of the said property since then. It is under those circumstances, that she sought for change in the mutation entries before the revenue authorities and it was rightly entered in her name. That plaintiffs have no right, title and interest in respect of the suit schedule property. What has been purchased by the plaintiff in Ex.P.4 sale deed 13.11.1981 is R.S.No.188/1. However, they have sought for inclusion of their names in respect of R.S.206/ 8 . The Tahasildar, Mudhol, rightly objected to entering of the plaintiff's name in the revenue records with regard to the suit schedule property. That the trial Court rightly dismissed the suit of the plaintiff, but the I Appellate Court was not right in decreeing the suit. He also pointed out that in Ex.P.4 i.e. the sale deed dated 10 13.11.1981 what has been mentioned is R.S.188/ 1 which is in fact what has been purchased by the plaintiff. Therefore, they have no right, title and interest in respect of the suit schedule properties. That the I Appellate Court lost sight of this fact and has erroneously decreed the suit of the plaintiffs. He also submitted that substantial question of law arises in this appeal which calls for intervention by this Court. 12. Per contra, learned Counsel for the respondents 1 to 5 and respondents 7 to 12 submitted that, respondent- 6 who is defendant-1 is the original owner of both R.S.No.188/1 as well as R.S.No.206/8; that he had sold the said properties to defendant-3 and to the husband of plaintiff-1 on different dates. That in the sale deed dated 23.4.1971 executed in favour of defendant-3 what has been mentioned was R.S.No.206/8 whereas what actually sold was R.S.188/ 1 . It is under those circumstances, defendant- 3 had filed O.S.265/1991. That the said suit was rightly decreed by the trial Court by judgment and decree dated 24.9.96, which has attained finality. That the plaintiffs 11 herein had not purchased R.S.188/ 1 . What had been purchased by them was R.S.206/ 8 . This is clear from the boundaries mentioned in Ex.P.4 which is dated 13.11.1981. Therefore, plaintiffs sought for declaration that they are the owners in possession of R.S.206/ 8 . That the I Appellate Court has rightly decreed the plaintiffs suit; that defendant-2 -the appellant herein, is a stranger with regard to the suit schedule property as well as R.S.188/ 1 . In fact ,it is defendant-1 who was the original owner of both the suit items and he alienated the same on different dates to different persons. Therefore, the I Appellate Court was right in decreeing the suit of the plaintiff. 13. Having heard the learned Counsel on both sides, it is not in dispute that Ex.P.4 is the sale deed dated 13.11.1981 by which defendant-1 sold to husband of plaintiff-1 the property bearing R.S.No.206/8 measuring 1 acre 32 guntas having following boundaries: Towards East by: Land of Bailappa Haveri, Towards West by: Land of Santappa Mang, now land of Laxman Mang. Towards North by: Land of Basappa Madar, now land of LaxmappaMang, 12 Towards South by: Land of Santappa Madar, now land of Laxmawwa Madar. It is also not in dispute that in the said document the survey number has been mentioned as R.S.188/ 1 . It is an undisputed fact that O.S.265/1991 was filed by defendant-3 as against defendant-1 as well as the plaintiffs seeking a declaration that he is the owner in possession of R.S.No.188/1 measuring 1 acre 22 guntas. The said suit was decreed on 24.9.1996. Ex.P.10 is a copy of the decree passed in O.S.265/1991. The boundaries shown in the decree with regard to R.S.188/ 1 measuring 1 acre 22 guntas are as follows: Towards East: land of Rayappa Maranur, Towards West: Baragi road, Towards South: Land of Gadigeppa, Towards North: Land of Abdulsaheb Dasagirasaheb Pathan. 14. The fact that both the plaintiffs as well as defendant-1 were arrayed as defendants in O.S.265/1991 shows that there was a controversy between them having regard to the fact that in Ex.P.4 dated 13.11.1981 survey number was mentioned as R.S.188/ 1 . However, by virtue of judgment and decree dated 24.9.1996 passed in 13 O.S.265/1991 which has attained finality, it becomes clear that defendant-1 had sold to defendant-3 R.S.188/ 1 . In fact the boundaries of R.S.188/ 1 and the boundaries of R.S.206/ 8 are also mutually exclusive. There is no overlap and neither are the lands adjacent to each other. Defendant-1 who is the vendor of this land has categorically admitted that 10 years after the sale of R.S.188/ 1 , R.S.206/ 8 was sold to the husband of 1st plaintiff as per Ex.P.4. The boundaries stated in Ex.P.4 are not the boundaries which tally with R.S.188/ 1 as stated in the decree passed in O.S.265/1991. It is under those circumstances, that the I Appellate Court has held that the plaintiffs were entitled to a judgment and decree as sought by them since Ex.P.4 is a document of title which clearly establishes that what they have purchased was a piece of land within the boundaries stated in Ex.P.4. The erroneous mentioning of survey number in Ex.P.4 would not take away the right, title and interest of plaintiffs in the land that had been purchased by the husband of first plaintiff on 13.11.1981. It is also a settled position in law that 14 boundaries would prevail over the extent. It is an erroneous mentioning of survey number in Ex.P.4 in the instant case. Therefore, when the boundaries mentioned in Ex.P.4 have been admitted by the vendor of the said property, namely, defendant-1, who also admitted that R.S.188/ 1 has been erroneously mentioned in Ex.D.4, whereas it ought to be R.S.206/ 8 , the said evidence of defendant-1 clinches the issue. Therefore, the right, title and interest of the plaintiffs with regard to R.S.206/ 8 is established by virtue of Ex.P.4. 15. As far as the claim made by defendant-2-the appellant herein is concerned, she has stated that she is the owner in possession of R.S.No.206/8 by virtue of a partition which took place. In the first place, the 2nd defendant is not related to defendant-1. No independent evidence has been let-in by the appellant herein to show as to how she came in possession of R.S.206/2008 at a partition between her family members. No other document of title is produced with regard to R.S.No.206/8. Under the circumstances, the revenue entries made in the name of defendant-2 with regard to the suit schedule property was erroneous. The plaintiffs 15 are therefore justified in seeking rectification of the revenue entries, which are now standing in the name of appellant herein. The I Appellate Court, therefore, rightly decreed the suit of the plaintiff by setting aside the judgment and decree of the trial Court. This appeal does not raise any substantial question of law. The appeal is dismissed. Parties to bear their own costs. Sd/- JUDGE Sub/
